chunk_summary: Feeding strategies for infants under 2 years with chronic illnesses focus on energy-dense nutrition, tube feeding, food safety, and specialized diets to support growth and manage disease-specific needs.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding modifications for infants with chronic illnesses like CHD, HIV, and immunodeficiency.
Learn strategies for tube feeding and food safety in immunocompromised pediatric patients.
Recognize the role of specialized diets in metabolic disorders such as PKU and galactosemia.
Identify guidelines for growth monitoring and nutritional interventions in chronic pediatric conditions.
key_facts: Infants with CHD or chronic lung disease may require higher-calorie feeds (e.g., concentrated breast milk or formula).
Exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis is recommended for HIV-exposed infants to prevent mixed feeding risks.
Micronutrient supplementation is critical after 6 months for HIV-infected infants due to growth faltering risks.
Immunocompromised infants should avoid raw/undercooked eggs, meats, unpasteurized dairy, and high-risk street foods.
Specialized formulas are essential for metabolic disorders like PKU and galactosemia, requiring close collaboration with a metabolic team.
Frequent growth assessments are necessary across chronic conditions to ensure adequate nutrition and development.
reference_quote: Exclusive breastfeeding with maternal ART and infant prophylaxis is recommended for HIV-exposed infants to prevent mixed feeding risks. (WHO, HIV and infant feeding guideline update 2016)
document_key_topics: Feeding strategies for chronic pediatric conditions
Nutrition in HIV-exposed or infected infants
